sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a top horizontal bar with blue/sky blue elements (possibly a cursor/player), a left panel with purple markers on gray, and a right panel with a 3x3 grid of alternating gray/white blocks. Clicking on grid blocks had no effect (board_changed=False). Movement actions (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT) are valid - need to test if something moves.
- Plan: Try movement actions to see if the blue/sky blue element in the top bar moves like a cursor.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 2 of about 36 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board changed! Looking at the nodes after RIGHT:
- Node 5: color=S (was b), pixels=8, cols [39-40] - was blue, now sky blue
- Node 6: color=b (was S), pixels=8, cols [41-42] - was sky blue, now blue
So the sky blue and blue elements on the right side of the top bar swapped colors! The sky blue "cursor" moved from cols 41-42 to cols 39-40 (moved left by 2 columns). Wait, but I pressed RIGHT...
Let me re-examine. Initially:
- Node 5: color=b, cols [39-40] 
- Node 6: color=S, cols [41-42]
After RIGHT:
- Node 5: color=S, cols [39-40]
- Node 6: color=b, cols [41-42]
So the sky blue moved from position 41-42 to 39-40, which is LEFT (toward the gray bar). But I pressed RIGHT...
Hmm, maybe the sky blue is at the RIGHT end and pressing RIGHT moves it left (toward the center)? Or maybe the cursor is the sky blue one and it moves in the opposite direction?
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe there are TWO cursors - one on the left (sky blue in the blue box) and one on the right (sky blue next to blue). The right-side cursor moved left when I pressed RIGHT.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Left side: Node 2 (b, blue) contains Node 3 (S, sky blue) - the sky blue is INSIDE the blue region
- Right side: Node 5 (now S, sky blue) and Node 6 (now b, blue) - they're adjacent
So on the right side, the sky blue moved from the rightmost position (cols 41-42) to the left position (cols 39-40). This means pressing RIGHT made the sky blue cursor move LEFT on the bar. That's counterintuitive...
Unless the cursor represents something that moves toward the center when you press RIGHT. Or maybe the bar works like a slider where RIGHT moves the indicator toward the left end.
Let me try pressing RIGHT again to see if it keeps moving, and also try LEFT to see the opposite effect.
Actually wait - I should also check if the left-side cursor (node 3, sky blue inside node 2) changed. Looking at the output, node 2 and node 3 are still at the same positions with same colors. So only the right-side cursor moved.
Let me try pressing RIGHT more times to see the full range of movement, and also try LEFT to reverse it.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT movement caused the sky blue element on the right end of the top bar to swap with the blue element - the sky blue cursor moved from cols 41-42 to cols 39-40 (leftward by 2). The left-side blue/sky blue box didn't change. The cursor seems to move along the bar.
Plan: Test more RIGHT presses to see the full range, then try LEFT to reverse. Need to understand the cursor mechanics and goal.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me track the cursor positions more carefully
# First, let me check the before/after of the last transition
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

def get_node_by_color_and_region(seg, color, row_min, col_min):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == color:
            b = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            if min(rows) >= row_min and min(cols) >= col_min:
                return n
    return None

# Compare right-side elements before and after
print("=== BEFORE RIGHT ===")
for n in before_seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'S'] and n['pixels'] <= 16:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

print("=== AFTER RIGHT ===")
for n in after_seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'S'] and n['pixels'] <= 16:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Now let me try pressing RIGHT several more times to trace the full path
print("\n=== Testing multiple RIGHT presses ===")
for i in range(5):
    action(['RIGHT'])
    # Find sky blue on right side
    for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S' and n['pixels'] == 8:
            b = n['boundary']
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            print(f"  After RIGHT #{i+2}: sky blue at cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
            break
    if last_action_result.get('done') or last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
        print("  Level completed or done!")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
